questionText: Prove that the least value of $a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta + c$ (with $a, b > 0$) is $c - \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$, and state the value of $\sin(\theta + \alpha)$ where it occurs.

steps.description: Write the varying part in R-form
steps.explanation: The R-form collapses the two oscillating terms into one sine of amplitude $R$.
The constant $c$ simply shifts the whole thing vertically.

steps.description: State the range of the sine
steps.explanation: The sine is bounded between $-1$ and $1$ for every angle.

steps.description: Scale by R and shift by c
steps.workingLatex: c - R \le R\sin(\theta + \alpha) + c \le c + R
steps.explanation: Multiplying by the positive $R$ and adding $c$ gives the range of the full expression.
The minimum of the expression is its lower bound.
The least value is attained exactly when the sine reaches its minimum of $-1$, completing the proof.
